utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced comprehensive, high-quality defect action plans that address all six evaluation criteria. Both plans correctly classify the functional defect, identify and quarantine the three escaped downstream units, recommend a lot hold for LOT-2024-0831-B, propose concrete poka-yoke mechanisms (go/no-go gauges, visual reference cards), specify detailed traceability data requirements, and quantify FPY impact. utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed all steps successfully and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that satisfy all 7 evaluation criteria. Both agents correctly applied 5S dimensional scoring with evidence linkage, categorized lean wastes by taxonomy, connected the step-6 skip to the under-torque defect rate, structured the kaizen event with measurable targets and role-specific teams, proposed concrete visual management solutions, linked cycle time variance to root causes, and framed the training gap as a systemic Sustain failure. The quality difference is marginal: wi_skills produced a slightly more structured S4 scoring (1/20 — 5%) and a cleaner summary dashboard with explicit stop/do-immediately action categories, while wo_skills produced a more detailed kaizen prep document (22,908 bytes vs 17,300 bytes) with a more explicit FPY target framing. Overall, both runs are effectively equivalent in meeting the evaluation criteria, with wi_skills showing marginally better 5S framework rigor and wo_skills showing marginally more comprehensive kaizen package detail. The skill did not produce a clearly superior qualitative gap in this case.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully in 5 steps, produced the required output file, and passed all 3 verifier checks.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, detailed documents that performed comparably on most evaluation criteria. Both correctly quantified the takt time gap (J1.1), identified specific work elements (J1.2), addressed andon protocol (J1.3), proposed shift-executable countermeasures (J1.4), and flagged the ergonomic risk (J1.6). Neither document explicitly stated the standard work change approval requirement (J1.5), which is the most notable gap shared by both. The wo_skills output was actually slightly more detailed in quantitative impact analysis (explicit takt efficiency percentages, unit output at 56s and 58s cycle times) and included a more structured verification checklist. The wi_skills output was slightly more action-oriented in framing. Overall, the two outputs are nearly equivalent in quality, with both exceeding the baseline expectation. The expected quality gap between skill-equipped and baseline agents did not materialize in this run — wo_skills performed at the same level as wi_skills, suggesting the underlying model is capable of applying lean manufacturing reasoning without the skill prompt in this instance.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that satisfy all 7 evaluation criteria. Both files include per-dimension 5S scores with specific evidence linkage, lean waste taxonomy categorization, explicit connection of step 6 skipping to torque defect data as a standard work failure, kaizen event structure with measurable FPY targets and role-specific team composition, concrete poka-yoke/visual management proposals, cycle time variance linked to specific root causes, and systemic framing of the training gap with a specific certification mechanism recommended. The wi_skills output is marginally more structured (e.g., explicit 'in scope / out of scope' framing, a plant manager kickoff message, and a detailed 30-day follow-up plan with named deliverables), but the quality gap between the two runs is smaller than anticipated by the test design. Both outputs read as shop-floor lean practitioner assessments rather than generic consulting reports. Overall: wi_skills is slightly more rigorous and complete, but wo_skills is also a strong performer on all scored items.
